Identity.History:
Socrates was an Athenian philosopher of the 5th century BC and one of the defining figures of Greek intellectual history. Born in Athens around 469 BC, he was the son of Sophroniscus, traditionally a stonemason, and Phaenarete, a midwife.
He served as a hoplite in the Peloponnesian War and was remembered for physical endurance and courage, but his lasting battlefield was the Athenian agora. Socrates wrote nothing. His thought survives through later authors, especially Plato and Xenophon, who portray him questioning citizens, poets, craftsmen, politicians, and ambitious young men about justice, courage, piety, virtue, and knowledge. His method was not formal teaching so much as relentless examination: he exposed contradictions, claimed awareness of his own ignorance, and urged Athenians to care for the soul more than wealth or reputation. This made him magnetic to students and irritating to powerful men.
After Athens' defeat by Sparta and the trauma of oligarchic coups, Socrates' associations with controversial aristocrats made him politically suspect. In 399 BC he was tried for impiety and corrupting the youth, convicted, and sentenced to death. Refusing escape, he drank poison and became a lasting symbol of moral courage, philosophical integrity, and civic conscience for later generations everywhere afterward.
